name: scarce emerald
shortDescription: A scarce emerald with a deep garden-green heart, cut to catch light like sunlight trapped under leaves. Jewelers prize it for noble settings, but old traders watch for the faint inner clouds that prove it was born in patient stone, not glass.
flavorText: “Emeralds do not shine like fire. They shine like a secret kept alive.”

name: scarce sapphire
shortDescription: A scarce sapphire of calm, commanding blue, the sort of stone that makes silver look colder and gold look more disciplined. It carries the quiet authority of deep water: beautiful, expensive, and unwilling to explain itself.
flavorText: “A sapphire does not beg for attention; it assumes the room will come around.”

name: fabled ruby
shortDescription: A fabled ruby with a red so full it seems less cut than wounded into shape. Merchants handle it with theatrical calm, because a stone like this can turn a polite appraisal into a family dispute before supper.
flavorText: “Rubies are bought with coin and paid for again in envy.”

name: fabled white diamond
shortDescription: A fabled white diamond of hard, merciless brilliance, throwing clean sparks even in poor light. It has the unsettling purity of something that has never cared who owned it, only who could afford to carry it next.
flavorText: “A diamond remembers no hand, but every hand remembers the diamond.”

name: gold crown
shortDescription: A gold crown with enough weight to remind the wearer that ceremony is never entirely comfortable. Its rim is shaped for power, pageantry, and the difficult art of looking certain while everyone nearby is counting successors.
flavorText: “A crown is just a ring that learned to command witnesses.”

name: gold crown with cushion
shortDescription: A gold crown resting on a small cushion, presented as though it has already judged the room worthy of silence. The cushion softens the display, but not the message: this is authority arranged for purchase, inheritance, or trouble.
flavorText: “The cushion is for the crown. The discomfort is for everyone else.”

name: gold crown with gem
shortDescription: A gold crown set with a proud central gem, bright enough to make even polished coin look like servant metal. It is less subtle than a plain crown, but subtlety rarely survives near thrones for long.
flavorText: “Power wears gold; vanity asks for a stone in the middle.”

name: fabled aqua crystal
shortDescription: A fabled aqua crystal with the color of shallow sea over pale sand, cool to the eye even before it touches the palm. Sailors and shrine-keepers both claim it calms tempers, though jewelers prefer the simpler truth that it sells beautifully near water.
flavorText: “Some stones look wet enough to forgive thirst.”

name: fabled black crystal
shortDescription: A fabled black crystal whose surface drinks lamplight and returns only a thin, watchful gleam. Buyers are drawn to it for mourning jewels, forbidden charms, and the private pleasure of owning something that seems to refuse explanation.
flavorText: “Black crystal is not dark because it lacks light. It is dark because it keeps it.”

name: fabled blue crystal
shortDescription: A fabled blue crystal with a clear, ringing color that suggests winter sky seen through enchanted glass. It suits scholars, navigators, and anyone who wants their wealth to look thoughtful rather than merely loud.
flavorText: “Blue crystal sells best to people who call desire clarity.”

name: fabled brown crystal
shortDescription: A fabled brown crystal with warm earth tones, smoky depths, and tiny golden hints that appear only when turned slowly. It lacks the arrogance of brighter stones, which is why experienced buyers often trust it more.
flavorText: “Brown crystal does not dazzle strangers; it rewards the patient hand.”

name: fabled cyan crystal
shortDescription: A fabled cyan crystal flashing between blue and green, bright as a clear river over white stone. It has a lively, almost mischievous color, making it popular with enchanters who want their work to look harmless before it is tested.
flavorText: “Cyan is the color magic chooses when it has not decided whether to heal or bite.”

name: fabled gray crystal
shortDescription: A fabled gray crystal with quiet facets and the restrained gleam of storm clouds before rain. It is favored by judges, oath-witnesses, and merchants who believe the most expensive objects should not have to shout.
flavorText: “Gray crystal is for buyers who want mystery without theatrics.”

name: fabled emerald crystal
shortDescription: A fabled emerald crystal, greener and stranger than a cut emerald, with light moving inside it like leaves stirred behind glass. Garden mages, court jewelers, and collectors all argue over whether it is a mineral, a relic, or a very expensive temptation.
flavorText: “Where emeralds suggest gardens, emerald crystal suggests the garden is awake.”

name: fabled indigo crystal
shortDescription: A fabled indigo crystal, dark at the edges and violet-blue at its core, as if dusk had been pressed into a sharp shape. It feels suited to astrologers, locked studies, and deals concluded after the sensible guests have gone home.
flavorText: “Indigo crystal belongs to the hour when lamps are lit and judgment weakens.”

name: fabled green crystal
shortDescription: A fabled green crystal with a clear herbal glow, cleaner and colder than common jade. It is the sort of stone healers admire, poisoners admire differently, and merchants keep wrapped until the right buyer says too much.
flavorText: “Green crystal never says whether it means remedy or venom.”

name: fabled magenta crystal
shortDescription: A fabled magenta crystal with a bold, theatrical color that refuses to behave like a respectable jewel. Court performers, scandalous nobles, and charm-workers love it because even a small piece looks like the beginning of a rumor.
flavorText: “Magenta crystal enters a room before its owner does.”

name: fabled red crystal
shortDescription: A fabled red crystal burning with a sharper, glassier fire than ruby, beautiful in the way a warning lantern is beautiful. It makes plain gold look dangerous and dangerous people look better prepared.
flavorText: “Red crystal is what flame might buy if flame carried coin.”

name: fabled pink crystal
shortDescription: A fabled pink crystal with a warm blush, sweet at first glance and surprisingly bright under a jeweler’s lamp. It is beloved for courtship gifts, reconciliation offerings, and apologies that need expensive assistance.
flavorText: “Pink crystal says what pride refuses to write.”

name: fabled crimson crystal
shortDescription: A fabled crimson crystal, darker and more solemn than red, with the look of old wine held against a sunset. It has a ceremonial gravity that makes buyers lower their voices without knowing why.
flavorText: “Crimson crystal does not sparkle; it pronounces.”

name: fabled white crystal
shortDescription: A fabled white crystal, pale as moonlit ice and clear enough to make flaws feel like moral failings. Temples prize it for clean symbolism; traders prize it because symbolism often pays in advance.
flavorText: “White crystal is innocence after it has hired a guard.”

name: fabled yellow crystal
shortDescription: A fabled yellow crystal bright with honeyed light, cheerful enough to seem lucky and rare enough to make luck negotiable. It suits festival crowns, shrine lamps, and anyone who wants wealth to look warm instead of severe.
flavorText: “Yellow crystal catches sunlight and charges it interest.”

name: gold necklace with blue gem
shortDescription: A gold necklace set with a blue gem, balancing warm metal against a cool, steady center. It feels made for formal dinners, winter portraits, and buyers who want elegance with just enough distance to keep admiration polite.
flavorText: “Gold flatters the throat; blue stone keeps the compliment composed.”

name: gold necklace with green gem
shortDescription: A gold necklace holding a green gem like a polished leaf caught in sunlight. It has the generous look of vineyard wealth, orchard dowries, and houses that prefer their luxury to suggest fertility rather than conquest.
flavorText: “A green gem on gold makes prosperity look almost natural.”

name: gold necklace with orange gem
shortDescription: A gold necklace set with an orange gem that glows warmly against the chain, lively as firelight on polished cups. It is not the most solemn jewel, but it is hard to wear one and seem dull.
flavorText: “Orange stone is for people who want the feast remembered.”

name: gold necklace with purple gem
shortDescription: A gold necklace with a purple gem at its center, made for people who enjoy the old friendship between luxury and authority. The piece carries itself like court gossip: rich, controlled, and never entirely innocent.
flavorText: “Purple on gold is not decoration. It is a claim.”

name: gold necklace with red gem
shortDescription: A gold necklace set with a red gem bright enough to turn a simple collarbone into an announcement. It suits weddings, victories, dangerous apologies, and all occasions where restraint has already been dismissed.
flavorText: “A red gem does not complete an outfit; it starts a conversation.”

name: gold necklace with white gem
shortDescription: A gold necklace with a white gem that shines cleanly against the warmth of the chain. It has the look of vow gifts and temple patronage, where wealth is expected to behave as if it were virtue.
flavorText: “White stone on gold is how vanity dresses for confession.”

name: silver necklace with blue gem
shortDescription: A silver necklace set with a blue gem, cool enough to look refined even when the clasp is plain. It has a winter-water grace that makes it popular with singers, scribes, and quiet heirs not yet ready for gold.
flavorText: “Silver and blue make modesty look deliberate.”

name: silver necklace with green gem
shortDescription: A silver necklace with a green gem, fresh and bright without the heavy promise of gold. It feels suited to spring fairs, small betrothals, and merchants who know a graceful gift can travel farther than a grand one.
flavorText: “Green on silver is a hopeful thing, and hope sells well before planting season.”

name: silver necklace with orange gem
shortDescription: A silver necklace set with an orange gem, cheerful against the pale metal and warmer than its price might suggest. It is the kind of piece a traveling performer buys after one very good evening and wears for luck afterward.
flavorText: “Some jewels are bought to look rich; this one is bought to look alive.”

name: silver necklace with purple gem
shortDescription: A silver necklace with a purple gem, courtly in color but restrained in cost. It lets clerks, minor nobles, and ambitious apprentices borrow a little dignity without having to explain where the gold came from.
flavorText: “Purple dreams bigger than silver, which is why they get along.”

name: silver necklace with red gem
shortDescription: A silver necklace carrying a red gem with a clear, bright flash, bolder than the chain that holds it. It is popular with festival dancers and anyone who wants passion to look affordable but not cheap.
flavorText: “Red on silver is a spark asking permission from moonlight.”

name: silver necklace with white gem
shortDescription: A silver necklace with a white gem, pale on pale, elegant in a way that rewards a closer look. It is often chosen for quiet ceremonies, winter gifts, and people who prefer grace to arrive softly.
flavorText: “Silver whispers. White stone makes sure the whisper is heard.”

name: agate ring
shortDescription: An agate ring with bands of earthy color curling through the stone like miniature landscapes. It is not a court jewel, but it has the dependable charm of something worn daily by a person with steady hands.
flavorText: “Agate is for those who prefer their beauty with a memory of dirt.”

name: notable black onyx ring
shortDescription: A notable black onyx ring, smooth and dark enough to make the finger seem more deliberate. It suits mourners, magistrates, duelists, and anyone who has learned that silence can be ornamented.
flavorText: “Onyx says nothing, which is why serious people trust it.”

name: market brass ring
shortDescription: A market brass ring polished to a friendly shine, more cheerful than precious and all the better for it. It is bought for fairs, first gifts, traveling disguises, and hands that want a little gold color without a gold problem.
flavorText: “Brass knows it is not gold, and still enjoys the sunlight.”

name: market bronze ring
shortDescription: A market bronze ring with a warm brown shine and the sturdy look of old tools. It feels less like luxury than a small personal token, the sort of ring that survives work, rain, and several bad decisions.
flavorText: “Bronze is what affection wears when it expects to be useful.”

name: ceremonial ring
shortDescription: A ceremonial ring made for vows, offices, guild rites, or the formal ending of arguments everyone will continue privately. Its value lies in the moment it witnesses, which is why owners become nervous when it goes missing.
flavorText: “A ceremonial ring is a small circle drawn around a large promise.”

name: clay ring
shortDescription: A clay ring, humble and fragile, shaped more by intention than wealth. Children make them, pilgrims bless them, lovers exchange them when coin is scarce, and occasionally they outlast grander promises by being too simple to resent.
flavorText: “Clay breaks easily; that is why honest hands treat it gently.”

name: market copper ring
shortDescription: A copper ring from a market tray, warm-colored, affordable, and quick to take the character of the hand that wears it. It may green the skin in damp weather, but it also makes a small gift possible before courage fades.
flavorText: “Copper rings are bought before speeches improve.”

name: coral ring
shortDescription: A coral ring carved from sea-born red branches, carrying the memory of reefs into a human shape. Coastal buyers favor it for protection, while inland buyers mostly enjoy owning something that once belonged to water.
flavorText: “Coral is the sea learning to wear a finger.”

name: diamond ring
shortDescription: A diamond ring with a clean, hard flash that makes the setting feel secondary no matter how fine it is. It is bought for vows, victories, inheritance, and the dangerous hope that permanence can be worn.
flavorText: “A diamond ring promises forever, then waits to see who can afford it.”

name: emerald ring
shortDescription: An emerald ring with a green center deep enough to draw the eye before the hand has finished moving. It suggests gardens, old houses, and fortunes that prefer to be inherited rather than explained.
flavorText: “Emerald on the hand makes even a lie sound well-bred.”

name: notable glass ring
shortDescription: A notable glass ring, cleverly made and prettier than common sense expects. It is not pretending to be a great jewel; its delight is in catching color, light, and the buyer who appreciates craft over pedigree.
flavorText: “Glass is honest about being fragile. That alone makes it rarer than some gems.”

name: gold ring with cyan stone
shortDescription: A gold ring set with a cyan stone, bright as clean water poured over sunlit metal. It has an easy charm that suits travelers, sea merchants, and people who want a jewel to look lucky without looking solemn.
flavorText: “Cyan stone on gold looks like a good omen with a receipt.”

name: gold ring with magenta stone
shortDescription: A gold ring with a magenta stone, vivid enough to make nearby jewels seem shy. It is made for bold hands, dramatic gifts, and buyers who believe taste should never be mistaken for caution.
flavorText: “Magenta is not a color for hiding the hand.”

name: gold ring
shortDescription: A plain gold ring, smooth, valuable, and dangerously easy to underestimate. Without a gem to distract from it, the metal itself does the speaking: warm weight, clean promise, and no unnecessary witness.
flavorText: “A plain gold ring is where ornament ends and intention begins.”

name: gold ring with blue stone
shortDescription: A gold ring set with a blue stone, joining warm metal to a cool and composed flash. It is a reliable choice for formal gifts, careful courtship, and anyone who wants affection to arrive properly dressed.
flavorText: “Blue stone steadies gold before gold says too much.”

name: gold ring with green stone
shortDescription: A gold ring with a green stone, small enough for daily wear but rich enough to be noticed at the counting table. It carries the pleasant suggestion of gardens, money, and promises made before witnesses.
flavorText: “Green stone on gold is prosperity pretending to be romance.”
